claim: Basic RAG fails in practice because it's a one-shot retrieval: the agent gets context once and can't reason about whether it's sufficient or needs to search differently; agentic RAG wraps retrieval as tools the agent can use strategically
actionable: Stop treating RAG as a preprocessing step; instead, expose retrieval functions as tools in your agent framework so the agent can call them multiple times, with different queries, and intelligently decide when it has enough context
source_title: The Future of RAG is Agentic - Learn this Strategy NOW

claim: Three core pain points with standard RAG: wrong text returned from vector search, LLM ignores the retrieved context, and logic that works in theory breaks in practice—agentic RAG solves these by adding agent reasoning
actionable: When debugging RAG failures, don't just tweak chunk size or embedding models; instead, add agent tools to list available document URLs and fetch full pages, letting the agent reason about which documents to retrieve rather than relying on vector similarity alone
source_title: The Future of RAG is Agentic - Learn this Strategy NOW

claim: For document chunking at scale, preserve semantic boundaries: don't split code blocks, paragraphs, or sentences mid-way; detect and respect these structures so each chunk is self-contained information
actionable: When chunking documents for RAG, implement overlap-aware splitting that identifies code blocks (``` markers in markdown), paragraph breaks, and sentence endings; adjust chunk boundaries to respect these structures rather than splitting at fixed byte counts
source_title: The Future of RAG is Agentic - Learn this Strategy NOW

claim: Store both embeddings and structured metadata (URL, title, summary, chunk number) in your RAG database; this enables agentic RAG where the agent can list URLs, reason about which to fetch, and pull full pages without relying solely on vector search
actionable: Design RAG schema with columns: embedding (for vector search), content, url, title, summary (LLM-generated for each chunk), chunk_number, source, metadata; use metadata filtering to segregate knowledge for different agents in one table
source_title: The Future of RAG is Agentic - Learn this Strategy NOW

claim: Use LLM-generated titles and summaries for each chunk; the agent can use these to decide whether to fetch a full page without reading all content, dramatically improving reasoning about what documentation to retrieve
actionable: After chunking documents, batch-call your LLM to generate a title and summary for each chunk (e.g., first 1000 chars of chunk); store these in your DB alongside content; expose them to the agent so it can decide to fetch pages based on summaries
source_title: The Future of RAG is Agentic - Learn this Strategy NOW

claim: Agentic RAG agents succeed with tools for: (1) list_documentation_pages—return all available URLs so agent knows where to look, (2) fetch_page_content—retrieve full content from specific URL for in-depth answers
actionable: Implement two core agent tools: list_docs() returns distinct URLs with metadata; fetch_content(url) returns all chunks for that URL joined together; expose these as callable tools in your agent framework with docstrings explaining when to use each
source_title: The Future of RAG is Agentic - Learn this Strategy NOW

claim: Superbase is preferable to Quadrant for RAG because Superbase offers both vector (embeddings) and structured data (URLs, titles, metadata) in one database; Quadrant only handles embeddings, forcing you to use two systems
actionable: For production RAG with agentic features, use Superbase (or similar SQL+vector DB) unless you need extreme vector search speed; the ability to store and query metadata alongside embeddings in one system is worth the slight speed tradeoff
source_title: The Future of RAG is Agentic - Learn this Strategy NOW

claim: Metadata filtering (e.g., where source='pydantic-docs') lets you store knowledge for multiple agents in one table without overlap or confusion—segment by source, time, or other attributes
actionable: When building multi-agent systems, add a 'source' field to all RAG chunks; when retrieving, filter: SELECT * WHERE source='current-agent-source'; this lets you scale to many agents without separate databases
source_title: The Future of RAG is Agentic - Learn this Strategy NOW

claim: When basic RAG fails to retrieve enough context (e.g., for complex examples requiring multiple page sections), agentic RAG with URL listing and page fetching gives the agent ability to pull full documentation pages instead of relying on vector similarity alone
actionable: Detect RAG failures using test queries: if basic RAG returns incomplete answers, add agentic tools; test the same query again; if agent can reason about which URL to fetch and succeeds, agentic RAG is the fix
source_title: The Future of RAG is Agentic - Learn this Strategy NOW

claim: Crawl4AI enables fast parallel web scraping optimized for LLMs (returns markdown) vs traditional crawlers; this is the foundation for building knowledge bases from documentation at scale
actionable: Use Crawl4AI (open source) instead of manual web scraping or traditional crawlers for building RAG knowledge bases from docs; it returns clean markdown, supports parallel multi-URL crawling, and is LLM-friendly
source_title: The Future of RAG is Agentic - Learn this Strategy NOW

claim: Pydantic AI framework makes agentic RAG implementation straightforward: define agent with dependencies (DB client), expose retrieval functions as tools with @agent.tool decorator, agent calls tools as needed
actionable: Use Pydantic AI to build agentic RAG: define your retrieval functions as tool-decorated methods on your agent; write docstrings explaining when/how to call them; the framework handles agent orchestration and tool calling
source_title: The Future of RAG is Agentic - Learn this Strategy NOW

claim: You can expand agentic RAG by creating separate knowledge bases for different content types (e.g., one for examples, one for API reference, one for code repo) and giving the agent tools to reason about which to search
actionable: For complex domains, build multiple RAG stores (examples KB, reference KB, code KB); expose separate search tools for each; let the agent reason about which store to query based on user intent; this is more effective than one massive knowledge base
source_title: The Future of RAG is Agentic - Learn this Strategy NOW

claim: Streamlit provides a lightweight way to build chat UIs for agents; handle streaming with message formatting functions that convert agent output to Streamlit-compatible format; maintain conversation history in agent message state
actionable: For RAG agent frontends, use Streamlit with streaming output; convert agent messages to Streamlit chat_message format; maintain full conversation history so agent can reference prior turns without re-retrieving
source_title: The Future of RAG is Agentic - Learn this Strategy NOW
